segments.s603-g41: This is a simple past transitive clause: subject (جهاندار شاه, 'the sovereign king') comes first, then the definite direct object marked by را (آبکش را), and finally the past verb سپرد ('handed/entrusted'). را marks آبکش as the definite object. The compound جهاندار شاه is an honorific/title + noun used as the subject.
segments.s603-g42: بشد here functions as a narrative/cohesive particle meaning 'so it came about / then' (classical usage), followed by the personal name لنبک as the subject. از راه گنجی is a prepositional phrase ('by/along the road, for a treasure'), and ببرد is the verb 'to take/carry' (here describing Lenbak's action). Word order is narrative particle → subject → oblique phrase → verbal action.
segments.s603-g43: ازان پس is an adverbial phrase ('thereupon/after that'), placing this action after the previous one. براهام را خواند is a transitive clause: object (براهام marked by را) + خواند meaning 'called/summoned'. The conjunction و links the reporting verb گفت ('said'), introducing reported speech.
segments.s603-g44: This is the reported question/exclamation. که‌ای (که + vocative/particle -ای) introduces the content of what was said ('that / “O…” / “what”'). در کمی گشته is a perfect/participial expression: در + کمی ('in lack/smallness') + گشته (perfect participle 'have become / grown'), and با خاک جفت is a figurative phrase 'paired/joined with the dust' (an idiom for being humbled or fallen). Together it asks/rhetorically states that one has been reduced to dust.
segments.s603-g45: This is a rhetorical question. چه introduces 'what/how' in an exclamatory sense, and گویی here is the second-person present/subjunctive sense 'you say' (classical usage) introducing the reported thought; که introduces the subordinate content. پیغمبرت is the object 'your prophet' and چند زیست is a short clause 'how long he lived' (چند = 'how many/how long', زیست = past tense 'lived').
segments.s603-g46: Another rhetorical question: چه بایست 'how ought one' (چه + بایست = 'how/what should one do'), چندی here means 'for how long / for a while', and به زشتی گریست is an infinitival/nominal phrase 'to weep over ugliness' (به + noun 'زشتی' = over/for ugliness; گریست = 'to weep/lament'). The clause asks how long one should grieve over badness/ugliness.
segments.s603-g47: سوار آمد is a simple past clause 'a horseman/rider arrived'. The conjunction و connects to گفت, which here functions as the speech verb 'said', followed by با من سخن ('spoke with me' / 'said to me' or 'speak with me'). In classical narrative the ordering 'arrived and said' is a common reportive sequence.
segments.s603-g48: ازان is anaphoric/adverbial 'from that / thereupon'. داستانهای گشته is a genitival noun phrase: داستانهای 'stories/tales' + گشته as a perfect participle 'that have passed/been' (i.e., bygone), and کهن 'ancient' qualifies them. The whole phrase means 'those old tales that have long since passed.'
segments.s603-g57: سزا نیست is a fixed predicative phrase meaning “it is not fitting/you do not deserve.” زین is an archaic/poetic form (~از این) meaning “from/than this” and combines with بیشتر “more” to give “more than this.” مر ترا is a compact dative/possessive sequence: مر = “from me/to me” (here ‘from me’) and ترا = “you” (accusative/benefactive), with را (following) marking emphasis/definiteness — so the whole chunk means “you do not deserve more than this from me.”
segments.s603-g58: درم is the coin (dirham). مرد درویش را is the noun phrase 'the poor/dervish man' with را marking it as the (indirect) object/recipient of giving. سرِ ترا is ‘your head’ (سر + possessive suffix ت = ترا) with را again for emphasis/definiteness; the clause is a compact, inverted way of saying that the dirham given to the poor man is what is due to you (i.e. ‘the poor man's dirham is yours’).
segments.s603-g59: به marks the indirect object ('to/for'), and ارزانیان here denotes the recipients (‘the needy/poor’ or those to whom things are cheap/given) — a collective/poetic plural. داد is the past tense ‘gave.’ چیزی که بود is a free/relative clause meaning ‘that which there was’ or ‘whatever (he) had.’ Together: ‘He gave whatever he had to the needy.’
segments.s603-g60: خروشان is an adverbial participle/adverb meaning ‘roaringly/tumultuously.’ همی is the older continuous/progressive particle (still/was) used before the past verb رفت ‘went.’ مرد جهود is the subject phrase ‘the Jew-man’ (جهود here = Jews, archaic), so the clause means ‘the Jew went off, roaring.’
segments.s603-g17: بشد is the past/become form 'it became' (here: 'he became'). پاک‌دل is a compound adjective 'pure‑hearted'. تا functions as 'even/as far as' emphasizing extent. به + خان marks the indirect object 'toward/for the khan'. جهود is the archaic noun 'Jews' used as a recipient; the clause literally says 'he became pure‑hearted even to the khan and the Jews.'
segments.s603-g18: همه خانه is 'the whole household/house' (همه qualifies خانه). دیبا و دینار are listed possessions 'brocade and dinars'. بود is the past copula 'was' linking the possession phrase to the subject: 'the whole house had brocade and dinars' (literally: 'all the house brocade and dinars was').
segments.s603-g19: ز introduces a list 'of/from'. پوشیدنی is a deverbal/adjectival noun meaning 'things to wear' or 'garments'. هم is 'also' linking the next item. The second ز repeats the 'of/from' for the next nominal, گستردنی, 'things for spreading' (e.g. cloths, carpets).
segments.s603-g20: Again ز marks the category. افگندنی is an adjectival/verbal noun 'that which is to be cast/thrown' and pairs with پراگندنی 'that which is to be scattered/dispersed'; the conjunction و links them as parallel items in the inventory.
segments.s603-g21: یکی is the indefinite 'a certain' (introducing an item). کاروان‌خانه 'caravanserai/inn' is the noun; بود is the past copula 'there was'. و connects a second noun, سرای, which here names another kind of lodging/house — the clause lists buildings that existed. (Note: the provided sense options for سرای do not match its meaning here.)
segments.s603-g22: کزان is a relative/adverbial 'from which' or 'of which'. خانه is 'house'. بیرون 'outside'. نبودیش is a fused negative noun/phrase meaning 'there was not' / 'lack of it' (here: 'there was no [place] outside it'). جای 'place' is the head of the phrase; word order is somewhat inverted: 'from which house, a place outside of it did not exist.'
segments.s603-g23: Repeated ز begins a list 'of/from'. در here functions as a precious thing (classically 'pearl'), but the supplied sense options don't match that meaning (so index is set to -1). یاقوت is 'ruby'. هر گوهری 'every jewel' continues the enumeration; و coordinates the items.
segments.s603-g24: ز هر بدره‌ای means 'of each / from every moonlike gem' — بدره‌ای is a poetic word likening a jewel to the full moon (here: 'beauty/moonlike gem'). بر سرش 'upon his head' (سر + clitic ش 'his'). افسری is 'a diadem/crest' serving as the predicate: 'a diadem was upon his head' (i.e. he wore a diadem of every gem).
segments.s603-g49: This subordinate clause begins with the conjunction که introducing a general statement. هرکس که is a relative expression 'whoever', followed by دارد (present tense 'has') + فزونی (noun 'abundance, increase') and the verb خورد which here means 'befall/receive' in the sense 'receive an increase' — so the clause reads literally 'whoever has abundance receives (more)'. Word order is Subject (هرکس که) — Verb phrase (دارد فزونی خورد).
segments.s603-g50: This is the parallel clause: کسی کو ندارد همی پژمرد. کسی کو (an older relative construction: 'he who / anyone who') + ندارد (present negative 'does not have'), همی as a habitual/presentive particle 'continually/still', and پژمرد (verb 'wither'). The clause means 'anyone who lacks it continually withers.' Note کو is an archaic relative particle here linking کسی to ندارد.
segments.s603-g51: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. دست یازان is a compound participial/attributive phrase (literally 'hand-reaching' or 'those/that reaching with the hand') modifying the implied agent; ز خوردن 'from eating' uses ز (poetic 'from') plus the verbal noun خوردن; بکِش is an imperative 'pull/draw (back)'. The sense is an order: 'Now, withdraw your reaching hand from eating.'
segments.s603-g52: ببین is an imperative 'see/look'. زین is the demonstrative/nominal 'this saddle' (زین = از + این in poetry), سپس 'then/thereupon', خوردن here is again the verbal noun 'eating', and آبکش is a concrete noun 'colander/strainer'. The clause reads as a directive to look and to remove/notice the colander associated with eating from this saddle — the syntax stacks imperatives and nouns in quick poetic sequence.
segments.s603-g53: This is a prepositional/noun list introduced by the poetic preposition ز (here 'of/about' or 'concerning'). The items are joined by و in the usual coordinate order: 'سرگین' (dung), 'زربفت' (gold-brocade), 'دستار' (turban) and 'خشت' (brick). The phrase has no verb by itself and functions as the objects/topics picked up by the following clause.
segments.s603-g54: Main predicate phrase. بسی is an adverbial (classical/poetic) meaning 'much' or 'many'; گفت is the past tense 'he said/spoke' (here introducing what was spoken or contrived); با marks accompaniment 'with'; سفله modifies مرد ('the lowly/vile man'); کنشت is a noun meaning 'device/contrivance/stratagem' and here specifies what was produced/uttered. The sequence means roughly 'he devised/spoke many contrivances with the lowly man.'
segments.s603-g55: Word order is verb phrase with the coin first: درم (a dirham/coin) + داد (past 'gave'). The indirect object is expressed with دل را چهار — classical placement puts the numeral after the marked noun phrase: 'چهار' (four) applies to the amount given to 'ناپاک دل' ('the impure/unclean-hearted'). را is the definite/object marker attached to the recipient phrase.
segments.s603-g56: بدو = 'to him' (به + او) placed before the reporting verb; گفت is the past 'he said' introducing reported speech or a content clause. کاین is the classical combination (که + این) functioning like 'that/this' to introduce the content; را after کاین marks the thing spoken of as definite; تو سرمایه‌دار is the predicate: 'you [are] the سرمایه‌دار' (holder/owner of the capital). In short: 'He said to him, “You are the keeper/owner of this.”'
segments.s603-g25: "که" introduces an explanatory/subordinate clause ('that'); "دانند" is a poetic/historic form of 'to know' used here to report past ability ('knew / could'), with the agent following it; "موبد" is the agent 'the mobad (Zoroastrian priest)'; "مر" is the dative pronoun 'for me' (indirect object); "آن را شمار" is an object phrase 'that (آن) — (marked by را) — the counting/number (شمار)', i.e. 'to count that'. Word order is verb → subject → indirect object → object phrase because of the poetic narrative style.
segments.s603-g26: "ندانست" is the past negative of 'to know' ('did not know'); it continues the comment about the mobad's inability. "کردن" is the verbal infinitive 'to do' and here refers back to counting (i.e., 'to do/count it'); "بس روزگار" is a noun phrase 'much time' / 'a great many days' (literally 'enough/time'), indicating that performing the counting would require a long time. Together: 'he did not know — to do (the counting) would take too much time.'
segments.s603-g27: "فرستاد" is the past tense verb 'sent'; in this poetic order the verb comes before its agent. "موبد" is the agent 'the mobad' who issues the sending; "بدانجا" is the directional adverb 'to that place'; "سوار" is the thing/person sent, here 'a rider/horseman' (a mounted messenger).
segments.s603-g28: The omitted subject (the mobad) is understood. "شتر خواست" literally places the object 'camels' before the verb 'خواست' — 'he requested camels'; here "خواست" means 'asked/requested'. "از دشت جهرم" is a prepositional phrase 'from the plain of Jahrom'; "هزار" at the end is the numeral 'a thousand' modifying 'camels'.
segments.s603-g29: "همه بار کردند" uses the noun 'بار' ('load, cargo') with the light/auxiliary past verb 'کردند' to mean 'they loaded everything' ('همه' = all). "و" is the coordinating 'and'; "دیگر نماند" means 'nothing else remained' or 'no more was left' (نماند = 'remained/was left').
segments.s603-g30: "همی" is a classical/poetic adverbial marker indicating continued or iterative action (here 'then/thereupon' or 'so'); "شاددل" is an epithet 'cheerful' modifying the (implied) driver; "کاروان را" is the definite object 'the caravan' (را marks the object); "براند" is the verb 'drive' (here narrative past/remote form meaning 'drove' or 'set in motion'). The whole clause says that the cheerful person drove the caravan onward.
segments.s603-g31: "چو" introduces a temporal clause 'when'; "بانگ" is 'a cry/voice'; "درای" (literally 'possessing/wealthy') functions here as a title/descriptor — 'the treasurer' or 'the rich official' whose voice is heard; "آمد" is 'came'; "از بارگاه" is the source phrase 'from the court/palace'. Together: 'When the treasurer's cry came from the court...'.
segments.s603-g32: "بشد" here means 'it happened / there came to be' (classical narrative particle introducing an occurrence) and introduces the appearance of a man; "مرد بینا" is 'a clear‑sighted/discerning man' (مرد = man, بینا = sighted/discerning); "بگفت" is the past form 'he said'; "آن" is the demonstrative 'that' (referring to the matter just announced); "به شاه" is a prepositional phrase 'to the king' showing the addressee. So: 'a sighted man rose and said that to the shah.'
segments.s603-g33: که introduces a content clause ('that'). گوهر فزون زین is a noun phrase meaning 'a gem greater than this' — فزون is a comparative/augmentive adjective modifying گوهر, and زین is an archaic demonstrative 'this / than this' placed before the locative phrase. به گنج تو is a prepositional phrase 'in your treasury' (به + noun), and نیست is the negative copula 'does not exist' closing the clause.
segments.s603-g34: همان is an emphatic/demonstrative 'exactly/that very', مانده is the participle/adjective 'remaining', and خروار is the measure 'kharvār' (a large quantity). باشد دویست is a copular construction with باشد (here functioning as 'is/amounts to') followed by the numeral دویست; the verb precedes the numeral in this poetic order.
segments.s603-g35: بماند is a verb 'remained' (third-person narrative); اندران = 'among them' (an older/prepositional locative), followed by the subject/title شاه ایران 'the king of Iran'. شگفت is a predicative/adjectival word 'astonished' describing the king; word order places the verb before the locative and subject for poetic effect.
segments.s603-g36: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. راز دل is a genitive phrase 'the secret of the heart' (راز governed by دل), اندیشه‌ها is the plural object 'thoughts', and برگرفت is a simple past/transitive verb 'he took up / he drew (forth)'. The clause means he took thoughts out of his secret heart (i.e., he began to ponder or form counsels).
segments.s603-g37: که introduces an indirect/explanatory clause ('that' / 'how'). چندین modifies the verb and means 'many/several' or 'much', بورزید is the past tense of ورزیدن 'practised/exerted', and مرد جهود is a nominal phrase 'a man of exertions' or 'a hardworking man' (مرد = man, جهود here 'efforts'). The verb precedes the subject for poetic order.
segments.s603-g38: چو is a conditional/temporal 'when/if'. روزی here means 'livelihood/fortune' (the sense given is not literal but contextually 'sustenance'); نبودش = 'it was not / he did not have it' (negated existential with enclitic -ش for 'his'), ز ورزش = 'from skill/practice', and چه سود is a rhetorical question 'what benefit (is there)?' — together: 'if no livelihood came from skill, what good (is the exertion)?'.
segments.s603-g39: ازان = 'from that / of that'. صد شتروار is a quantified phrase 'a hundred shatrvār' (a poetic measure, here 'hundred camel-loads' or large lots); زر و درم = 'gold and dirhams' (gold and silver coin) listing the types of treasure. The whole is an enumerative phrase describing the treasure.
segments.s603-g40: ز (poetic) marks origin/partitive 'of / from'. گستردنیها is a plural noun formed with -ها meaning 'things to be spread/distributed' (i.e., goods meant for distribution). و از بیش و کم is an idiomatic addition 'and of more and less' meaning 'in amounts both large and small' — بیش = more/greater, کم = little/less; the construction lists variety in quantity.
segments.s603-g9: ببردند is past-tense plural ‘they took/led’. ز (= from) introduces the origin (ز ایوان = from the iwan/portico). به here marks direction ‘into/towards’. The two tokens راهام و را together mark the following phrase as the destination/target or topicalized object in this archaic surface (here functioning as the place/rooms they were taken into). Word order is verb — origin — direction/place, typical of narrative Persian.
segments.s603-g10: جهود here uses the archaic/poetic sense ‘Jews’ (not ‘efforts’). The adjectives بداندیش و بدکام follow the noun in this line and describe those Jews as ‘ill‑minded/malicious’ and ‘ill‑fated/unsatisfied’. The final را is the definite/object marker, marking this whole NP as what was led/taken.
segments.s603-g11: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when’. در بارگه رفت is a simple past clause ‘(he) went into the court’ (در = in, بارگه = royal court). بنشاندند is a past transitive ‘they seated/installed (him/them)’; the two clauses are adjacent so: ‘When he entered the court, they seated (him).’
segments.s603-g12: یکی here means ‘a certain’ (introducing an indefinite person). The adjective پاک‌دل precedes/qualifies مرد (a pure‑hearted man). را is the object/definite marker attached to مرد, and خواندند here is the transitive past ‘they called/summoned’ (not ‘read’). The whole NP is therefore the person they summoned.
segments.s603-g13: بدو گفت is the common classical phrase ‘he said to him’ (بدو = to him; گفت = said). رو is an imperative particle here meaning ‘go!’ or ‘turn to’ (an instruction). بارگیها is the target/place (plural of بارگاه ‘court/rooms’) and ببر is an imperative ‘take!’ — so the sequence is reported speech giving two imperatives: go/take them to the rooms.
segments.s603-g14: نگر is an imperative ‘see to it / watch’. تا introduces a purposive/subjunctive clause ‘so that’ or ‘in order that’. نباشی here functions as a negative subjunctive/jussive meaning ‘there be none / remain not’ (classical syntax uses subjunctive after تا for desired outcome). به جز is the fixed phrase ‘except’, and دادگر is ‘just / a just man/ruler’. Together: ‘See to it that there remain none except the just.’
segments.s603-g15: به خان is a directional phrase ‘to the khan/inn/house’. The second به + راهام repeats the directional/topic marking toward the rooms (راهام = the rooms/ways) and شو is an imperative ‘become/go’ (classical command form). بر گذار is a compound-like instruction ‘pass on / pass by’ (بر = on/upon; گذار used here in the sense of passing/setting down). The line strings imperatives: go to the khan, pass by the rooms.
segments.s603-g16: نگر again is imperative ‘look/see to it’. تا introduces purpose/result ‘so that’ and begins the embedded interrogative/relative clause چه بینی نهاده: چه = ‘what’, بینی = ‘you see’, نهاده = ‘placed/laid (down)’. The clause functions as object of نگر: ‘see what you find put (there)’, and بیار is imperative ‘bring (it)’. The sequence is therefore two commands: inspect and bring whatever is placed.
segments.s603-g1: برفت و بیامد is a paired past-tense sequence meaning ‘he went (off) and came (back)’; classical Persian often uses بَرفت (برفت) as simple past ‘he departed’. به ایوان خویش is a prepositional phrase with به + place (ایوان) and the possessive formed by خوی + enclitic ش = “his own,” so word order is Verb + direction + possessed location.
segments.s603-g2: همه شب همی ساخت درمان خویش: همه شب = ‘the whole night’ (time adverbial). همی before ساخت is the older continuous/imperfective marker meaning ‘kept on / continuously’; ساخت is past ‘made/constructed’ and درمان خویش is the direct object (درمان = remedy/treatment) with خوی + ش as the reflexive/possessive ‘his own’. Thus: ‘he kept devising his own cure all night.’
segments.s603-g3: پراندیشه is an adjective/epithet ‘full of thought / pensive’ modifying the (implicit) subject; آن شب = ‘that night.’ به ایوان بخفت uses به + place and بخفت (past of خفتن) = ‘he slept’ (classical past form). Word order places the time/epithet before the verb as a topical frame.
segments.s603-g4: بخندید = past ‘he laughed’; و links the clauses. آن راز با کس نگفت literally ‘that secret with anyone he said-not’ — نگفت here is the past negative of گفتن (‘did not tell’) and با کس (‘to/with anyone’) means ‘to no one’ in this negative context. The clause means he laughed but revealed the secret to nobody.
segments.s603-g5: به شبگیر چون تاج بر سر نهاد: به شبگیر (at nightfall / when night came — شبگیر as an adverbial/temporal expression), چون here = ‘when/as’. تاج بر سر نهاد uses بر + سر = ‘upon the head’ and نهاد (past of نهادن) = ‘placed/put’ — a simple past action: ‘he placed the crown upon his head.’
segments.s603-g6: سپه را سراسر همه بار داد: سپه را (سپَه + object marker را) = ‘the army (as definite object)’. سراسر همه are intensifying adverbials = ‘throughout / entirely.’ بار here = ‘burden / charge / responsibility’ and داد = past ‘gave’ — together ‘gave the charge/responsibility of the entire army’ (i.e. entrusted the army).
segments.s603-g7: بفرمود تا لنبک آبکش: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past ‘he ordered/commanded’. تا introduces a subordinate clause meaning ‘that / so that’ here, and لنبک is a proper name. آبکش is the role/title — literally ‘water-carrier / (lit.) sieve’ in the text used as an occupational designation: ‘ordered that Lenbak be (made) the water-bearer.’
segments.s603-g8: بشد پیش او دست کرده به کش: بشد is the verbal form of شدن ‘became/was made’ (here the result of the order: ‘he was made’). پیش او = ‘before him / in his presence’. دست کرده is a participial perfect construction ‘having put (his) hand’; به + کش indicates the object/manner — کش here is the verb ‘to pull’ (i.e. the bucket or water-drawing action). Together the clause describes his coming before the lord with his hand on (or to) the bucket.

sentences.s603-s21: The sovereign king handed over Abkash; then Lenbak set out to carry off a treasure along the road.
sentences.s603-s22: Thereupon he summoned Brahma and said, 'Have you, in your smallness, become joined with the dust?';
sentences.s603-s23: What do you mean—how long did your prophet live? How long should one weep over ugliness?
sentences.s603-s24: A horseman arrived and said to me, 'Tell me of those ancient, bygone tales.'
sentences.s603-s29: You deserve no more than this from me; the poor man's dirham is yours (your due).
sentences.s603-s30: He gave whatever he had to the needy; the Jew-man went off, roaring.
sentences.s603-s9: He became pure‑hearted, even toward the khan and the Jews; the whole house had brocade and dinars.
sentences.s603-s10: There were garments, things to spread, and things to cast or scatter.
sentences.s603-s11: There was a certain caravanserai and a saray; from that house there was no place outside of it.
sentences.s603-s12: Of pearls and rubies and every jewel, and from each moonlike gem he had a diadem upon his head.
sentences.s603-s25: For whoever has abundance is increased; anyone who does not have it withers.
sentences.s603-s26: Now, pull back your reaching hand from eating; look — from this saddle then take away the eater's colander.
sentences.s603-s27: He contrived many schemes with the lowly man concerning dung, gold-brocade, turbans, and bricks.
sentences.s603-s28: He gave four dirhams to the impure-hearted man and said to him, 'You are the keeper (holder) of this capital.'
sentences.s603-s13: That even the mobad did not know how to count that for me — to do so would take far too much time.
sentences.s603-s14: The mobad sent a mounted man there and asked for a thousand camels from the plain of Jahrom.
sentences.s603-s15: They loaded all the cargo and nothing else remained; the cheerful one then drove the caravan onward.
sentences.s603-s16: When the cry of the treasurer came from the court, a clear‑sighted man rose up and said that to the king.
sentences.s603-s17: That there is no jewel greater than this in your treasure; of it exactly two hundred kharvār remain.
sentences.s603-s18: The King of Iran remained amazed among them, and from the secret of his heart he drew up thoughts.
sentences.s603-s19: That, however much a man laboured, if no livelihood came from his skill, what good was it?
sentences.s603-s20: From that there were a hundred camel-loads of gold and dirhams, and items for distribution, in amounts both great and small.
sentences.s603-s5: They led the ill‑minded, ill‑fated Jews from the portico into the chambers.
sentences.s603-s6: When he went into the court they seated him; they summoned a certain pure‑hearted man.
sentences.s603-s7: He said to him, “Go and take (them) to the chambers; see that none remain except the just.”
sentences.s603-s8: Go to the khan and pass by the chambers; look and bring whatever you see placed there.
sentences.s603-s1: He went and returned to his own iwan; all night long he kept fashioning his remedy.
sentences.s603-s2: Thoughtful, that night he slept in the iwan; he laughed, but he told that secret to no one.
sentences.s603-s3: At nightfall he set the crown upon his head and entrusted the entire army to him.
sentences.s603-s4: He commanded that Lenbak be made a water-bearer; he stood before him with his hand on the bucket.
